title: Common Sense on Mutual Funds
subtitle: Impassioned Essays on Common Sense for Extraordinary Index Investors
author: John C. Bogle
authorBio: John C. Bogle (1929–2019) was an American investor, business magnate, and philanthropist who founded The Vanguard Group in 1974 and created the world's first index mutual fund in 1975. A Princeton philosophy graduate (A.B., 1951), his senior thesis 'The Economic Role of the Investment Company' foreshadowed his life's mission to put investors first. He led Vanguard for three decades, pioneering the no-load, low-cost, passively managed fund structure that revolutionized individual investing. He authored ten books, including Common Sense on Mutual Funds (1999, 2010), The Little Book of Common Sense Investing (2007), and The Clash of the Cultures (2012). Warren Buffett called him 'a hero to American investors.' He died at age 89 on January 16, 2019.
keyConcepts: The math of index fund victory (Cost Matters)
The craps game of active management
Performance chasing and why it always fails
Three sources of stock return: dividend yield, earnings growth, P/E change
Asset allocation as primary driver of portfolio returns (90%+)
Index fund superiority across equity, bond, and money market fund categories
Buy the haystack, not the needle
Speculation vs investment (Clash of the Cultures)
Zero-sum before costs, loser's game after costs
notableQuotes: Don't look for the needle in the haystack. Just buy the haystack.
The stock market is a giant distraction from the business of investing.
The winning formula for success in investing is owning the entire stock market, and doing so at the lowest possible cost.
The more the managers and brokers take, the less investors make.
In investing, you get what you don't pay for.
Time is your friend; impulse is your enemy.
The tyranny of costs consumes more than half of the return that investors could otherwise receive on their investments.
The stock market is a giant casino, and the odds favor the house — always and everywhere.
If you think the stock market is focused on making you rich, you will inevitably be disappointed.
The central problem in the mutual fund industry is that the investment advisor is working for the fund manager, not for you.
warrenBuffettQuote: Jack Bogle did more for individual investors than the entire rest of us put together. He has been a crusader, a voice of reason, and an example.
charlesMungerQuote: John Bogle has transformed the way people think about investing. He is a missionary, remarkably moral, impressive salesman for the exact right idea — low-cost indexing.
readability: Accessible; designed for individual investors and financial professionals; mathematical arguments use basic percentage arithmetic
